retrieved_docs.title: Cycloplegic and Noncycloplegic Refraction -- Function -- Tropicamide
retrieved_docs.content: This is a commonly used anti-cholinergic mydriatic drug. The drug has a powerful mydriatic effect, fast onset of action, and low side effect profile. Tropicamide is available at a dose of 0.5% and 1%. It works by blocking the muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. The 1 % preparation paralyzes the accommodation. The 0.5% strength produces mydriasis with slight cycloplegia. Tropicamide is implicated in mydriasis and cycloplegia, diagnosis, and short-acting mydriatic for pre and post-operative stages. The pupillary dilatation is less dependent on iris pigmentation. For premature infants combination of 2.5% phenylephrine and 5% is recommended due to less dilatation alone. [13] Characteristic Features of Cycloplegics and Mydriatics

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Precipitating angle closure in primary open-angle glaucoma patients, as well as the potential increase in intraocular pressure after mydriasis, is a frequent concern to many ophthalmologists. [25] However, multiple studies involving high-risk groups demonstrated that the incidence is relatively low, and the risk of underdiagnosing vision-threatening diseases should be prioritized. [26] The importance of getting a clear view of the fundus should not be undermined. Nonetheless, clinicians should carefully monitor and educate at-risk patients regarding what symptoms to look out for, such as headache and eye pain. Pupillary dilation should be avoided in fellow eyes of acute angle closure glaucoma until an open peripheral iridotomy is ensured. Tropicamide is a category C drug in pregnancy, and it is unknown whether it is excreted in human milk. Allergic contact dermatitis or allergic blepharoconjunctivitis due to mydriatics:

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Along with the active ingredient (tropicamide), the drop has multiple inactive ingredients. These include benzalkonium chloride (preservative), purified water, edetate disodium dihydrate, and boric acid. Hydrochloric acid and/or sodium hydroxide are used to adjust pH to 4.0 to 5.8. For refraction, one drop of tropicamide 1% is put in each eye 5 minutes apart. For examination of the fundus, 0.5% strength is an option. Tropicamide may dilate pupils in acute anterior uveitis, and one drop every 5 to 10 minutes should optimally dilate the pupil.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: There are no recommended diagnostic exams for monitoring the use of tropicamide. Pupillary dilation and dimness of near vision denote that the drug's action has started. If the pupil reacts to light, that means that the pupil is not fully dilated, and more time or more administration of tropicamide eye drops is needed for the maximal action to come.

retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Clinical Significance -- Antimuscarinic (from weakest to strongest)
retrieved_docs.content: Tropicamide: A muscarinic antagonist that is used to dilate the pupil and paralyze the accommodation reflex, commonly used for ophthalmic examinations. [103]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: A study of 23 healthy subjects demonstrated that pilocarpine had an insignificant impact on returning pupil diameter to baseline. Seventeen percent of subjects reportedly had a reduction in visual acuity, indicating that pilocarpine use is futile and possibly detrimental. [33] For these reasons, its use after tropicamide-induced dilation has not gained wide acceptance. Alternatively, an adrenergic blocking agent, such as dapiprazole (0.5% eye drop) or moxisylyte (thymoxamine), can be used. This class of drugs exerts its effect by causing relaxation of the dilator muscle. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Clinical Significance -- Antimuscarinic (from weakest to strongest)
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclopentolate: A muscarinic antagonist used for mydriasis and cycloplegia during eye examination and surgery. The antagonist has a longer duration of action compared to tropicamide. [104]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism for Cycloplegia
retrieved_docs.content: Accommodation becomes hindered when tropicamide blocks the muscarinic receptors of the ciliary body. Its cycloplegic effect can last anywhere from 4 to 10 hours, with the onset of action occurring within 20 to 30 minutes. [13] This leads to reduced near vision.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Administration requires the removal of contact lenses. This topical ophthalmic solution is instilled into the eye in droplet form. The lacrimal sac should be compressed with the fingertip for 2 to 3 minutes after administration to reduce systemic absorption and systemic adverse effects. The tip of the dropper should not touch any surface as it might contaminate the drug. In a child, ensure the drug does not get to their mouth. Hands require washing before and after instilling the drop. The individual should avoid driving and not engage in potentially hazardous activities during pupillary dilation. After pupillary dilation, sensitivity to light may occur, and sunglasses may be necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism for Dilation
retrieved_docs.content: As a parasympathetic antagonist, tropicamide exerts its dilatory effects by acting on the pupillary sphincter muscle to cause its relaxation. [10] Like other anticholinergic agents, tropicamide inhibits the parasympathetic drive, allowing sympathetic actions to dominate. [11] As the radial muscles of the iris (dilator pupillae), which are innervated by the sympathetic nervous system, are unaffected, they contract and cause the pupil to dilate. Its optimal effect occurs 25 to 30 minutes post-administration. [12] Typically, mydriasis reverses within 4 to 8 hours. However, it may take 24 hours for the mydriatic effect to wear off in some individuals. Weaker strength may cause mydriasis with little cycloplegia.

retrieved_docs.title: Cycloplegic and Noncycloplegic Refraction -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Tropicamide is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma and hypersensitivity to the drug. Tropicamide is linked with CNS disturbance in infants and children. It should be used carefully in the elderly and cases with increased IOP. Tropicamide locally causes a stinging sensation, and systemic absorption may result in confusion and hypersensitivity reaction in children. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Achieving a clinically effective pupil diameter of 6 mm is essential for viewing structures in the posterior segment. The most common indication for a comprehensive exam with pupillary dilation is screening for diabetic retinopathy. The nondilated exam gives the correct classification for the presence or stage of diabetic retinopathy in only  50% of cases. [2] Pupillary dilation is necessary for specific intraocular procedures such as cataract surgery to reduce intraoperative complications and for better exposure to the cataract during surgery. [3] Pupillary dilation is also necessary for retinal surgery and investigations of the posterior segment. The use of mydriatic agents such as tropicamide has, therefore, become ubiquitous in each optical setting. Tropicamide comes in two formulations, 0.5% and 1.0%. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Although tropicamide is a short-acting drug, and the frequency of associated adverse events is rare, there are certain instances when reversal of mydriasis is necessary, including decreasing the risk of angle-closure glaucoma, improving blurry vision, and decreasing recovery time for patient comfort. Presumably, a cholinergic agent like pilocarpine should be able to undo the effects of an anticholinergic drug. However, several studies have reported conflicting pupillary responses. [32] [33] [26] [34] At times, only a partial reversal was observed. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tropicamide is a safe drug for pupillary dilation before a comprehensive eye exam or ocular procedure. Pupillary dilation is also necessary for specific intraocular procedures such as cataract surgery to reduce intraoperative complications and for better exposure to the cataract during surgery. Pupillary dilation is also essential for retinal surgery and investigations of the posterior segment. The use of mydriatic agents such as tropicamide has, therefore, become ubiquitous in optical settings. This activity will review drug indications, mechanisms of action, side effects, and how an interprofessional approach can improve patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Eyecare specialists are interested in accommodation paralysis, also known as cycloplegia, when determining a patient's refractive error. Cycloplegic agents inactivate the ciliary muscle, so the patient cannot influence the refractive measurement. For example, an accurate evaluation is necessary before LASIK (laser-assisted in situ keratomileuses) surgery. In a 2017 meta-analysis, the more popular cycloplegic drug of choice, cyclopentolate, was compared to tropicamide. The research concluded that tropicamide is a viable alternative. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tropicamide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: An infrequently used option is spray application. Several studies have shown that using tropicamide in its vaporized form is just as effective in achieving increased pupil diameter but with less patient-reported discomfort. [16] [17] [18] In a randomized clinical trial, researchers concluded that administration via an ophthalmic insert, Mydriasert (containing 5.4 mg of phenylephrine hydrochloride and 0.28 mg of tropicamide), was safe and effective for use in neonates. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic mydriatics and cycloplegics are classified as a type of mydriatics and cycloplegics, agents used to dilate the pupil and temporarily paralyze the eye’s accommodative ability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine is classified as a mydriatic agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2710
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, a group of agents that produce pupil dilation. As mydriatic drugs, they are used in ophthalmic practice to achieve mydriasis for diagnostic examinations, therapeutic procedures, and the management of certain eye conditions, reflecting their shared pharmacologic role in facilitating ocular assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1262
retrieved_docs.content: Cyklopentolate is classified as a mydriatic, indicating that it is used to induce dilation of the pupil.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine, cyclopentolate, hyoscyamine and racemic adrenaline are all classified as mydriatic agents. As mydriatics, each of these substances produces dilation of the pupil by influencing the autonomic control of the iris, either through antagonism of parasympathetic receptors or stimulation of adrenergic pathways. This shared pharmacologic property makes them useful in ophthalmic examinations, diagnostic procedures and certain therapeutic situations where pupil dilation is required. Recognizing that these diverse compounds belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate mydriatics based on specific patient needs, onset of action, duration of effect and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 965
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics, ophthalmologic; DIAGNOSTIC AGENTS, OPHTHAMOLOGIC; MYDRIATICS AND CYCLOPLEGICS; and Anticholinergic mydriatics and cycloplegics are all classified as OPHTHALMOLOGICALS. The OPHTHALMOLOGICALS group represents a broad category of ophthalmic agents that can possess anti‑inflammatory, antibacterial, antifungal, and anesthetic properties. Consequently, OPHTHALMOLOGICALS is understood to encompass ophthalmic anti‑inflammatory agents, ophthalmic antibacterial agents, ophthalmic antifungal agents, and ophthalmic anesthetic agents. Recognizing that local anesthetic ophthalmic preparations, diagnostic ophthalmic agents, and mydriatic/cycloplegic drugs (including anticholinergic variants) belong to this unified class helps clinicians select appropriate medications for eye care, whether the therapeutic goal is pain control, infection prevention, inflammation reduction, fungal suppression, or pupil dilation and cycloplegia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, meaning they are pharmacologic agents used to dilate the pupil. As mydriatic drugs, they act on the iris muscles to produce pupil enlargement, which is valuable in ophthalmic examinations and certain therapeutic procedures. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and similar clinical applications in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: Mydriatics and cycloplegics serve as a broad parent category of ophthalmic agents, encompassing a range of drugs that affect pupil dilation and accommodation. Within this overarching group, the subclass identified as Ophthalmic Agents includes several specialized types of eye medications. One specific child of the Ophthalmic Agents category is Ophthalmic Anti‑Infectives, which are formulated to treat and prevent infections of the ocular surface and internal eye structures. This hierarchical organization highlights how mydriatics and cycloplegics provide the foundational classification, while Ophthalmic Agents and their child, Ophthalmic Anti‑Infectives, represent more focused therapeutic groups used in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2457
retrieved_docs.content: Methazolamide is classified within the therapeutic class of ophthalmic agents, indicating that its primary use is in ocular therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1628
retrieved_docs.content: Cykloplegika are used as mydriatics and cycloplegics, serving to dilate the pupil and temporarily paralyze accommodation during ophthalmic examinations and procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1960
retrieved_docs.content: Fysostigmin and isofluorofát are classified as members of the pharmacological group “Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics.” This category comprises agents that mimic parasympathetic activity, are employed in the treatment of glaucoma, and produce miosis (pupil constriction). By acting as cholinergic agonists or acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, these drugs increase aqueous humor outflow and lower intra‑ocular pressure, thereby helping to manage glaucomatous optic neuropathy. Recognizing fysostigmin and isofluorofát as part of this class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies for patients requiring parasympathomimetic or miotic effects, particularly in ocular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Acetazolamide, brinzolamide, dichlorfenamid, and dorzolamide are all members of the pharmacologic class of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, which function as antiglaucoma drugs and possess miotic activity. By inhibiting the enzyme carbonic anhydrase, these agents reduce the production of aqueous humor in the eye, thereby lowering intra‑ocular pressure and helping to control glaucoma. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic role make them important options in the management of elevated intra‑ocular pressure and related optic nerve damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: work by enhancing cholinergic activity, beta‑adrenergic blockers (e.g., befunolol, levobunolol) reduce aqueous production, and prostaglandin receptor agonists (e.g., omidenepag, omidenepag isopropyl) increase uveoscleral outflow. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antiglaucoma regimens and in appreciating how different drug classes complement each other within the comprehensive management of glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: agents that enhance aqueous humor outflow via cholinergic stimulation. MYDRIATICS AND CYCLOPLEGICS form a related group used for pupil dilation and cycloplegia. Overall, the structure interconnects cardiovascular pharmacology (beta‑blockade, vasodilation, lipid modification), platelet inhibition, and ocular therapeutics, illustrating how agents such as nicotinic‑acid derivatives serve both as peripheral vasodilators and lipid‑modifying drugs, how hypolipidemic agents represent a distinct lipid‑lowering group, and how ophthalmic agents integrate beta‑blockade, cholinergic stimulation, sympatholysis, and vasodilation to manage glaucoma, pupil dynamics, and related ocular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1966
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine, cyclopentolate, and hyoscyamine are all classified as mydriatics, a group of drugs used to induce pupil dilation. Mydriatics are pharmacologically related to racemic adrenaline, a non‑selective adrenergic agonist that also produces mydriasis. Although these agents share the common therapeutic purpose of dilating the pupil, they belong to different pharmacologic classes: ibopamine is a synthetic catecholamine derivative, cyclopentolate is an antimuscarinic compound, and hyoscyamine is a naturally occurring anticholinergic alkaloid. Recognizing that ibopamine, cyclopentolate, hyoscyamine, and racemic adrenaline all function within the mydriatic category helps clinicians choose the most appropriate agent based on factors such as onset of action, duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, thereby optimizing patient care in ophthalmic examinations and procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1265
retrieved_docs.content: Betaxolol and metipranolol are both members of the pharmacologic class described as “Beta blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics.” This class is itself defined as a type of antiglaucoma preparation and miotic, indicating that its constituent agents combine beta‑adrenergic blockade with ocular hypotensive and pupil‑constricting effects. Consequently, betaxolol and metipranolol are categorized as beta‑blocking agents that are also used as antiglaucoma medications and miotics, reflecting their role in reducing intra‑ocular pressure and managing glaucoma.
